coredata.dc:title: Flexible and transparent graphene-based supercapacitors decorated with nanohybrid of tungsten oxide nanoflakes and nitrogen-doped-graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Sial, Qadeer Akbar
Javed, Muhammad Sufyan
Lee, Young-Jae
Duy, Le Thai
Seo, Hyungtak
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the development of flexible and transparent (F-T) devices, including energy storage, is of interest for wearable electronics and emerging Internet of Things applications. However, it is challenging to secure the high-power density and high energy storing capacity of F-T energy-storing devices. Thus, our proposed strategy here is the combination of CVD graphene (as a charge collector) with a 2D-0D nanohybrid of tungsten oxide nanoflakes and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (WO3 NFs and NGQDs, respectively, as active materials providing multiple bonding types) to produce high-performance F-T supercapacitor electrodes. Our wet-synthesis and spray-decoration of the WO3 NFs/NGQDs nanohybrid are economical and suitable for large-scale and patternable processes. The fabricated F-T electrodes own a high specific capacitance of 117 F/g (at a scan rate of 50 mV/s) and excellent capacitance retention of 98.91% after 5000 charging-discharging cycles. Our developed symmetric supercapacitor devices show a better specific capacitance of 178.82 F/g (at 50 mV/s scan rate), capacitance retention up to 95.29% after 5000 charging-discharging cycles, along with power and energy densities of 360.11 W/kg and 15.79 Wh/kg, respectively. Overall, the obtained results show the potential of our proposed strategy for creating superior F-T energy storage and the usability of our symmetric supercapacitors for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene supercapacitor
Flexible
Transparent
Tungsten oxide
Nitrogen-doped quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of F-T supercapacitor electrodes and symmetric cells
Morphological and structural characterization of supercapacitor electrode

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

item-info.ce:copyright: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the fabrication process of WO3 NFs/NGQDs based flexible supercapacitor.
FE-SEM mages of the synthesized WO3 NFs. (a) Low and (b, c) high magnification FE-SEM images, (d, e) EDX elemental-mapping images (f) EDX results.
TEM images of: (a) NGQDs. (b) WO3 NFs/NGQDs. (c–f) Corresponding elemental-mapping images. (g) EDS results and the inset table show the quantitative values of elements in the WO3 NFs/NGQDs.
Structural characterization of WO3 NFs/NGQDs: (a) Powder X-ray diffraction pattern; (b–d) Deconvoluted spectrums of W4f, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively.

Electrochemical performances WO
3
NFs/NGQDs based supercapacitor electrode. (a) CV curves at different scan rates ranging from 50 to 300 mV/s. (b) Specific capacitance of the electrode as a function of scan rate. (c) GCD curves at the different current values ranging from 0.1 to 1 mA/g; (d) Nyquist plot (EIS) of the electrode; here, the left inset shows the enlarged plot at high frequencies and the right inset illustrates the equivalent circuit. (e) Cycling stability tests of WO3/NGQDs based electrode at a constant current density of 0.2 mA/g; insets represent the first 3 and last 3 cycles of GCD.

Electrochemical characterizations of WO3 NFs/NGQDs based symmetric supercapacitor device in two electrode system using acidic electrolyte (H3PO4/PVA): (a) CV curves of device at different scan rate ranging from 50 to 400 mV/s in potential window of −0.4 to 0.4 V; (b) GCD curves at different current density of in potential window of −0.4 to 0.4 V; (c) CV curves at different bending angles and (d) GCD curve at different bending angles.

(a) CV curves of device after 150 and 5000 cycles at scan rate of 50 mV/s, (b) GCD curves of device after 150 and 5000 cycles, (c) EIS spectra of device before and after 5000 cycles, (d) capacitance retention.

head.ce:title: Flexible and transparent graphene-based supercapacitors decorated with nanohybrid of tungsten oxide nanoflakes and nitrogen-doped-graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Systems Research, Ajou University, Suwon, 16499, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy Systems Research, Ajou University, Suwon, 16499, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Siyuan Laboratory, Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center of Vacuum Coating Technologies and New Energy Materials, Department of Physics, Jinan University, Guangzhou, 510632, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center of Vacuum Coating Technologies and New Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Siyuan Laboratory, Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center of Vacuum Coating Technologies and New Energy Materials, Department of Physics, Jinan University, Guangzhou, 510632, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Ajou University, Suwon, 16499, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Ajou University, Suwon, 16499, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, COMSATS University Islamabad, Lahore Campus, Punjab 54000, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, COMSATS University Islamabad, Lahore Campus, Punjab 54000, Pakistan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Energy Systems Research, Ajou University, Suwon, 16499, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the development of flexible and transparent (F-T) devices, including energy storage, is of interest for wearable electronics and emerging Internet of Things applications. However, it is challenging to secure the high-power density and high energy storing capacity of F-T energy-storing devices. Thus, our proposed strategy here is the combination of CVD graphene (as a charge collector) with a 2D-0D nanohybrid of tungsten oxide nanoflakes and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (WO3 NFs and NGQDs, respectively, as active materials providing multiple bonding types) to produce high-performance F-T supercapacitor electrodes. Our wet-synthesis and spray-decoration of the WO3 NFs/NGQDs nanohybrid are economical and suitable for large-scale and patternable processes. The fabricated F-T electrodes own a high specific capacitance of 117 F/g (at a scan rate of 50 mV/s) and excellent capacitance retention of 98.91% after 5000 charging-discharging cycles. Our developed symmetric supercapacitor devices show a better specific capacitance of 178.82 F/g (at 50 mV/s scan rate), capacitance retention up to 95.29% after 5000 charging-discharging cycles, along with power and energy densities of 360.11 W/kg and 15.79 Wh/kg, respectively. Overall, the obtained results show the potential of our proposed strategy for creating superior F-T energy storage and the usability of our symmetric supercapacitors for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Transparent and wearable electronics have proliferated in a wide range of applications for the sake of the development of Internet-of-Thing technology [1,2]. As a result, the demands of deformable, transparent, and high-performance energy storage devices increase rapidly nowadays [3,4]. Apart from possessing high energy storage capability, energy storage devices consisting of charge collectors, active materials, and electrolytes must be capable of operating in harsh mechanical conditions, in which they are deformed; for example, bent, stretched, or twisted [5]. Therefore, improving the mechanical stability and cycle life of energy storing devices is mandatory for wearable applications [6,7]. Indeed, these are the main challenges to fabricate flexible and transparent (F-T) energy devices and systems, in which the devices can be integrated with solar cells, nanogenerators, or photodetectors, as well as embedded in transparent displays.

It is worth noting that supercapacitors, owing fast charge-discharge capability, cyclic stability, and excellent power density, have emerged as a rational and promising candidate for use as portable energy-storing devices [8–10]. The electrochemical performance of supercapacitor is strongly dependent on the characteristics of active materials. Moreover, several active materials have been developed for F-T supercapacitors [11,12]. However, those F-T supercapacitors typically show poor cyclic stability, which hinders their practical application. Many researchers recently reported, transition-metal oxides are promising candidates as pseudocapacitors having high electrochemical performance and stability [13–17]. Among these oxides, tungsten oxide (WO3) has attracted considerable interest for use in supercapacitor, solar cell, photocatalysis, gas sensing, and lithium-ion batteries [12,18,19]. However, metal oxides generally have many synthesis methods and good transparency but low electrical conductivity, electrochemical reactivity, and sustainability to mechanical deformations which are the major impediments to its use in the fabrication of high-performance F-T supercapacitors. So, many attempts have been made to overcome those impediments of using metal oxides, including tungsten oxides (WO3).

In particular, WO3 composite with carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon fibers, graphene sheets carbon aerogel, and graphene to form composites has proved to be effective in enhancing the capacitive performance of WO3 [20–22]. Recently, WO3-graphene composites have emerged as a useful material for the fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors since their specific capacitance (143 F/g) is considerably higher than that of WO3 (32.04 F/g) [23]. Furthermore, WO3 nanoflowers covered with reduced graphene oxide (RGO) have shown excellent specific capacitance (495 F/g) and high energy density [24]. However, these composites have the disadvantage of low stability in solid electrolytes, short cycle lifetime, and poor rate performance. Consequently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are highly crystalline with a size of several nanometers, are good candidates to improve the electrochemical performance of WO3 and can help to overcome the shortcomings of current methods [25]. Several reports have shown the usefulness of GQDs for the fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors [26–28]. Besides, the excellent electrochemical properties of GQDs make them useful for various applications such as photovoltaic devices, sensors, photocatalysis, and lithium-ion batteries [29]. As reported, the excellent electrochemical performance of GQDs comes from their unique surface properties with abundant active sites for the redox reactions, leading to better interactions between them with the electrolyte molecules. That certainly improves the cyclic stability, specific capacitance, and rate performance of supercapacitors based on GQDs. Furthermore, many studies have shown that doping GQDs with nitrogen can enhance the electrochemical properties of GQDs because of the faradic and interface properties in the aqueous electrolyte [30]. Nonetheless, securing the good electrical connections between GQDs and their transparency in visible light region is challenging. Besides, making nanohybrids based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and transition-metal oxide nanostructures has not been explored fully for F-T applications.

In this work, we present the synthesis of graphene (through chemical vapor deposition, CVD), WO3 nanoflakes (NFs, through a solvothermal method), and NGQDs (through a hydrothermal method) for making F-T supercapacitor devices at low temperatures and low cost. CVD-graphene-covered PET substrates are prepared before spray-coating of WO3 NFs. After that, NGQDs decoration is performed with the same technique. Besides structural and chemical composition characterizations, the developed electrodes based on the WO3 NFs/NGQDs nanohybrid are tested to evaluate their electrochemical performance and specific capacitance. Furthermore, F-T symmetric supercapacitors were fabricated and measured to estimate the power and energy densities and we finally demonstrate from all electrochemical tests that the WO3 NFs/NGQDs nanohybrid has a potential for practical use.

Poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA, Mw ~ 15,000) powder, ammonium persulfate ((NH4)2S2O8, Mw = 228.20), urea powder (Mw = 60.02), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, Mw = 9000–10,000), phosphorus acid (H3PO4) and a copper etchant were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Copper foil (Alfa Aesar), anhydrous citric acid (Samcheon Chemical, South Korea), and sodium tungstate dihydrate (Mw = 329.85, Junsei Chemicals, Japan) were purchased.

The copper foil was treated with ammonium persulfate (1 M aqueous solution) for a short period to remove native oxides and then cleaned with deionized (DI) water. The CVD growth of graphene was carried at 1000 °C (ramping speed of 20 °C/min) at a constant pressure of 0.5 Torr in the presence of pure H2 (50 sccm). Methane gas (CH4, 99.99%) was introduced as a source of carbon for 30 min with a flow rate of 5 sccm. The sample was then cooled in the same hydrogen flow. The synthesized graphene on copper foil was supported by PMMA (10 wt% in toluene), which was spin-coated at 3000 rpm for 30 s and annealed for 20 min at 80 °C. After etching copper foil by the ammonium persulfate solution, the PMMA/graphene layer was transferred to a polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate, dried at 50 °C for 1 h, and then transferred to acetone bath for removing PMMA. Finally, the graphene/PET sample was annealed at 70 °C for 10 min and 110 °C for 1 h.

WO3 NFs were obtained by a reported synthesis method [31] with a small modification. Briefly, 30 ml of sodium tungstate (Na2WO4) aqueous solution was added to 200 ml of H2SO4 in a dropwise manner until the pH reached 0.8. The solution color turned yellow after the solution was mixed thoroughly. The mixture was aged at 30 °C with gentle stirring for 24 h and then cleaned by centrifugation with DI water (until its pH was about 5–6). The cleaned mixture was kept in an oven at 60 °C overnight to obtain a powder (i.e., nanoflakes). The obtained powder of WO3 was additionally calcined at 500 °C for 2 h to form highly crystallized WO3 NFs, which would be dispersed in methanol (3 mg/ml) by sonication.

NGQDs were synthesized based on a modification of a reported method [32]. At first, 0.76 and 0.96 g of citric acid and urea, respectively, were dissolved in 20 ml of DI water and stirred gently for 30 min. Then this solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was heated to 140 °C for 4 h. The obtained brown solution was cleaned by centrifugations with DI water and then ethanol. The final NGQDs dispersion in ethanol was weighted (ca. 0.3 mg/ml).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of F-T supercapacitor electrodes and symmetric cells
ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate was utilized to fabricate F-T supercapacitor devices. The as-synthesized CVD graphene (1 × 2 cm) was transferred on a PET substrate. WO3 NFs were spray-coated for 30 s on a predefined area (1 × 2 cm) of the graphene/PET sample by using a shadow mask. The samples were preheated at 80 °C on a hotplate. Subsequently, NGQDs was spray-coated on the confined area on these samples which were then annealed at 80 °C for 1 h to improve adhesion. The copper tape was used as a connection between the electrodes and the external measurement system. The active mass on the PET substrate was calculated to be 0.5 mg/cm2 after the substrate was weighed by using an electronic weight balance. In the fabrication of the symmetric supercapacitor device, both positive and negative electrode material was the same, and both electrodes were face-to-face sandwiched by hot-pressing at 100 °C. A gel electrolyte of polyvinyl alcohol and phosphorus acid (PVA-H3PO4) was employed between positive and negative electrodes. The gel electrolyte was prepared by dissolving 2 g of PVA in 20 ml of DI water (at 80 °C) and then adding 4 g of H3PO4 (without heat) with stirring until a clear solution. The gel electrolyte was cast on the graphene-based on the electrode and partially dried at 100 °C for 3 h. An illustration of fabricating supercapacitor devices is shown in Fig. 1 .

The X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima III diffractometer, USA) analysis of the synthesized products was performed. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan) analysis was carried out. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) analysis and Raman spectroscopies (Jobin Yvon, HORIBA, Japan) was applied for chemical identification.

The electrochemical performance of the prepared electrode and supercapacitor device was analyzed using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements and impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using Ivium-n-stat potentiostat (IVIUM TECHNOLOGIES, Netherland). The as-prepared WO3 NFs/NGQDs based single electrode was used to estimate its electrochemical performance using three-electrode configuration in 0.5 M H3PO4 electrolyte, and the as-fabricated WO3 NFs/NGQDs based electrode was used as a working electrode, a platinum mesh worked as a counter electrode, and calomel electrode as a reference electrode. The performance of the fabricated supercapacitor electrode was evaluated by estimating the specific capacitance of the fabricated electrode and corresponding energy and power densities by using the following expressions [33,34].

where C
s and C
cell (in farad per gram) are the specific capacitances of a supercapacitor electrode and a symmetric cell, respectively; m
e and m
c (in grams) are the mass of active materials and the total mass of a cell, respectively; v (volt per second) represents the scan rate; V
f
-V
i is the change in the applied potential window; E (in watt-hour/kilogram) and P (in watt/kilogram) are the energy and power densities of a supercapacitor cell, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of supercapacitor electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical response and stability of a supercapacitor are directly influenced by the morphology and active sites of the electrode surface. Thus, electron microscopy measurements were carried out, as presented in Fig. 2 . At different magnifications, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images in Fig. 2a-c clearly show the shape of as-synthesized WO3 nanoflakes having a relative size of 100–500 nm and an average thickness of 10–20 nm. Fig. 2d, e show the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping results of WO3 NFs which reveal the presence of tungsten (W) and oxygen (O) elements. Along with that, the result in Fig. 2f also confirms that the synthesized nanoflakes consist of pure WO3. Fig. 3 a displays transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of NGQDs which have a sphere-like shape with a relative size of 10 nm. Fig. 3b shows the decoration of NGQDs on the surface of WO3 NFs. However, as the size of synthesized NGQDs was too small, they could not be captured and observed on the WO3 NFs clearly. Thereby, scanning tunneling electron microscopy (STEM) was used to reveal the presence of NGQDs on WO3 NFs as confirmed in Fig. 3(c–f). Furthermore, the EDX analysis of the selected area shows a small atomic fraction of carbon (C) arising from NGQDs that indicates the quantity of NGQDs in the nanohybrid layer is very small. As a result, nitrogen dopants localized on the partial surface of NGQDs was too little to map in the EDX measurement in Fig. 3g. Regardless, XPS analysis provides a clear evidence of true existence of nitrogen in NGQDs as discussed later.

Fig. 4 a shows XRD results of WO3 NFs and WO3 NFs/NGQDs nanohybrid. Here, the as-synthesized WO3 NFs were crystalline and had a monoclinic structure without any additional peak (JCPDS no. 752072). The XRD pattern remained unchanged after the decoration of NGQDs on the surface of WO3 NFs, indicating that NGQDs did not affect the monoclinic structure of WO3 NFs and were only physically attached to the surface of WO3 NFs. During the XRD measurements, fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) was used as a substrate; the FTO peaks are also marked. For further understanding on the chemical properties and electronic states in the WO3 NFs/NGQDs surface, XPS analysis was performed. In Fig. 4b, the deconvoluted XPS spectrum of W4f showed spin-orbital doublet peaks of W4f5/2 and W4f7/2 at binding energies of 37.40 and 35.38 eV, respectively. The synthesized WO3 NFs showed a dominant +6 oxidation state in W4f5/2 and W4f7/2 [20]. The +5 oxidation state was also confirmed by further deconvolution, which resulted in the appearance of peaks at 33.65 and 36.39 eV. In Fig. 4c, the deconvoluted C1s peak contains four components attributed to C–C (284.33 eV), C–N (284.67 eV), C–OH (286.35 eV) and O–CO bonds (288.07 eV); the presence of these functional groups made NGQDs hydrophilic in nature, enabling more chemical interactions with aqueous electrolytes. In Fig. 4d, the deconvoluted XPS spectrum of the N1s peak shows three components at 399.33, 400.01, and 401.10 eV corresponding to pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N–C bonds, respectively. That confirms the existence not only of N dopants in GQD but also the various functional groups in NGQDs, offering a higher electrochemical reactivity [35].

The optical properties of the synthesized NGQDs were studied using UV–Visible spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information, SI). It was observed that the synthesized NGQDs showed absorption peaks at wavelengths of 240 and 345 nm that are in good agreement with previously reported study [36]. Here, the peak at 240 nm was caused by the π-π* transition, while that at 345 nm appeared because of nitrogen dopants in NGQDs; The physical origin of the optical transition can be explained by the affinity of nitrogen atoms for electrons led to delocalization of electrons in graphene, resulting in the π-π* transition [37]. These types of transitions occur by transferring charges from the edge of the sp2 carbon domains or from functional groups of surfaces to the π* orbitals of sp2 carbon domains [38]. Moreover, the sharp and narrow peaks indicate the quantum confinement effect of our NGQDs, making them up with a wide and sharp band gap. The optical properties of the fabricated supercapacitor device were analyzed, and it was observed that the optical transmittance of the fabricated supercapacitor device exceeded 70% in the visible spectrum, as displayed in Fig. S2 (SI).

The mechanical property of the supercapacitor device was examined through cyclic bending measurements (3000 cycles at each bending radius), as described in Fig. S3 (SI). The resistance of the electrode changed with an increase in the bending radius. Briefly, the obtained results showed that the electrode was highly stable for a wide range of bending radii, from 75 to 20 mm. Compared to the initial resistance of the electrode (~1.4 kΩ), the change in its resistance at a bending radius of 20 mm (bending more than 90°) was about 30% (still lower than 2 kΩ).

The electrochemical properties of our supercapacitor electrodes and cells using the active WO3 NFs/NGQDs nanohybrid on graphene/PET samples were investigated through CV, GCD and EIS measurements in a three-electrode system. Here, a phosphoric acid electrolyte (0.5 M H3PO4) and a Pt counter electrode were used. Fig. S4 (SI) shows CV results of bare WO3 NFs and WO3 NFs/NGQDs at a scan rate (v) of 50 mV/s in an optimized potential window (between −0.8 and −0.4 V). The CV curves of both electrodes have an rectangular shape without visible prominent redox peaks, implying the electrochemical double layer capacitive characteristics [39]. The integral area of the CV curve of the WO3 NFs/NGQDs electrode shows a remarkable enhancement as compared to that of the WO3 NF electrode, verifying the high reactivity and electrochemical performance of NGQDs as expected. Fig. 5 a shows the CV results of the WO3 NFs/NGQDs electrode at various scan rates (v = 50–300 mV/s). With an increase in the applied scan rate, the rectangular shape of its CV curves is retained as the result of the enhanced surface and capacitive properties of the electrode with the presence of NGQDs [40]. In addition to the CV shape change, the current response also linearly increases with the scan rate, indicating that the fabricated electrode possesses an excellent charge storage capacity and fast charge transportation at the electrode/electrolyte surface due to additional functional groups of NGQDs, as fore-mentioned in Fig. 4c, d. Fig. 5b shows calculated values of the specific capacitance of the nanohybrid electrode which are 198.15 F/g at v = 1 mV/s, 178.25 F/g at v = 5 mV/s, 158.65 F/g at v = 10 mV/s, 138.34 F/g at v = 20 mV/s, 117.19 F/g at v = 50 mV/s, 101.25 F/g at v = 100 mV/s and 90.17 F/g at v = 200 mV/s. The CV at small scan rates is shown in Fig. S4(b) in supporting information. Here, the active material loading (m = 0.5 mg/cm2) weighted during the electrode fabrication was applied for all electrochemical calculations.

GCD profiles of the nanohybrid supercapacitor electrodes in the selected potential window (−0.8 ~ −0.4 V) with different current densities (mA/g) are presented in Fig. 5c. The shape of the GCD plot is nearly triangular and shows good symmetry with an almost linear slope at high current densities. This indicates the electrochemical double layer characteristics of the electrode, and these results are consistent with the CV results. It can be observed that the IR drop was small in the discharging cycles, suggesting that the I–V response was very fast due to the low internal resistance offered by the electrode. The main reason for IR drop relies on the internal resistance (of electrode), properties of electrode-electrolyte interface, and/or the fast charge transportation [41].

For further understanding, EIS was performed because EIS analysis can extract information about the electrochemical interaction of the electrode and electrolyte of a supercapacitor. As shown in Fig. 5d, the EIS result, measured in a frequency range of 10–100 kHz and at a voltage of 0.1 V, is presented in the form of a Nyquist plot. Here, the equivalent circuit is illustrated in the inset, in which R
s is a series resistance formed by the interactions of the electrolyte and active materials; R
1 and R
2 stands for charge transfer resistances (R
ct ) caused by probably both faradaic charge transfer process and double layer capacitance; Q is the remaining stored charge from the ion diffusion mechanism to the active material; and W is the Warburg diffusion coefficient indicating the frequency-dependent ion diffusion of the electrolyte to the electrode [42]. In this figure, it was observed that in the low-frequency region, the slope was steeper, which is referred to as Warburg impedance (W) [43]. The steeper slop of line in the low-frequency region also indicated that the electrolyte was well diffused inside the supercapacitor electrode and hence improved its electrochemical properties. In the high-frequency region of the Nyquist plot, the radius of a semicircle was small, indicating that electrons and ions were transported easily into the electrode with a low Rct. Overall, the results confirmed the fast charge and high charge storing capacity of our electrode. Cyclic stability is considered an essential parameter for the evaluation of the performance of the supercapacitor electrode. The stability of the WO3 NFs/NGQDs electrode was determined by continuously operating the electrode for 5000 GCD cycles with an applied current density of 0.5 mA/g, as shown in Fig. 5e. As observed, the fabricated electrode owns remarkable specific capacitance retention up to 98.91% after 5000 testing cycles; the inset of Fig. 5e shows the first and last three cycles of the GCD stability test.

For the evaluation of the performance of the WO3 NFs/NGQDs electrode in practical applications, a symmetric supercapacitor device was fabricated with two nanohybrid electrodes. CV tests were performed with two electrode systems (scan rates in the range of 50–400 mV/s and the operating potential window between −0.4 and 0.4 V), as presented in Fig. 6 a. Evidently, with an increase in the applied scan rate, the CV curve retained its shape, and it was observed that the current was linearly increased with the applied scan rate, confirming the good conductivity between the electrolyte-electrode of supercapacitor device. The fabricated supercapacitor device showed the specific capacitance of 178.82 F/g at 50 mV/s and at lower scan rate it showed specific capacitance of 326.105, 281.08, 255.93 and 215.86 F/g at scan rates of 1, 5, 10 and 20 mV/s respectively. The CV at small scan rates is shown in Fig. S4(c) in supporting information. Fig. 6b displays the GCD curves of the supercapacitor device at various current densities. To examine the deformation suitability of the fabricated F-T supercapacitor device for practical wearable application, the device was bent at different angles (0°, 45°, 120° and 180°). Under these bent conditions, CV and GCD tests were performed as presented in Fig. 6c, d, respectively. The shapes of the CV and GCD curves of the bent device were slightly changed from those of the undeformed device, probably because the contact geometry between the two electrodes was altered by the deformation. To check the device stability and repeatability, CV and GCD cyclic tests were performed. After the first 150 cycles and after 5000 cycles, CV and the GCD curves are presented in Fig. 7 a,b, respectively. The fabricated supercapacitor device showed reliable stability for 5000 cycles, and there was a small change in the GCD and CV curves. Fig. 7c shows EIS spectra taken before and after the stability test. Here, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the supercapacitor device was calculated ca. 9 Ω, which is increased as compared to the fabricated electrode. The stability of the supercapacitor device was analyzed on the basis of capacitance retention over 5000 cycles, as shown in Fig. 7d. The specific capacitance was maintained for 2500 cycles, and after 3000 cycles, it was reduced to 95.29%, which was maintained up to 5000 cycles. As calculated, our supercapacitor device achieved high energy and power densities (15.79 Wh/kg and 360.11W/kg respectively). More importantly, the electrochemical performance of our device is maintained well under bending deformations as shown in Fig. S5 (SI). Altogether, the stable and high electrochemical performance of our device can be attributed to not only the high porosity but also the large surface area of the network of NGQDs and WO3 NFs.

In summary, our F-T supercapacitor electrodes and devices based on WO3 NFs and NGQDs were successfully developed. Utilization of economical synthesis methods (e.g., hydrothermal, solvothermal and spray-coating) to prepare the nanohybrid is beneficial and scalable for industrial production. Furthermore, combination of low dimensional materials and CVD graphene enabled our devices to be highly flexible and transparent in the visible light spectrum. The synthesized nanohybrid was characterized by using various techniques and the results reveal the importance roles of the porous network and large surface area of our active material for better electrode-electrolyte interactions. Electrochemical measurements showed that the supercapacitor device had a high specific capacitance of 178.82 F/g with power and energy densities (360.11 W/kg and 15.79 Wh/kg, respectively) and possessed excellent cyclic stability and its specific capacitance decreased by 4.71% after 5000 cycles. These results are promising for practical wearable energy applications. Importantly, the results also confirmed the potential of the multi-dimensional hybridization of nanomaterials for superior flexible energy storing devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interests to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are very grateful for the financial support from the Basic Science Program (2018R1D1A1B07050008) and Korea Research Fellowship program (2018H1D3A1A02074733) through the National Research Foundation (NRF) of the Ministry of Science and ICT, Republic of Korea. This work was also aided by Ajou University.

ce:section.ce:para:
